coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots from carbonized walnut shells: Structural evolution, fluorescence characteristics, and intracellular bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Chaoge
Shi, Yanni
Li, Min
Xing, Malcolm
Wu, Qilin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted tremendous attention for their prominent fluorescence, excellent stability, and outstanding biocompatibility. Herein, we report a facile method to prepare CQDs from walnut shells, which mainly consist of natural cellulose. After carbonization of the walnut shells and acid treatments, zigzag and armchair edges of CQDs with an average size of 3.4nm were revealed by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. Consistent with the (100) planes of graphitic carbon, the lattice spacing distance of these CQDs was 0.258nm. The photoluminescence behaviors related to the size/shape and corresponding edge-state. The CQDs exhibited green fluorescence when the excitation wavelength ranging from 360nm to 460nm. Meanwhile, the CQDs showed hydrophilic, pH-sensitive and up-converted photoluminescence properties. We also found pH and solvent had reversible effects on CQDs' photoluminescence. The CQDs were then tested with live-cell fluorescent images for the potential intracellular probes. Raman mapping technique was also applied to detect the CQDs' distribution in vitro and clarify the entering procedure of CQDs into cells besides confocal microscopy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Walnut shells
Cellulose
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent properties
Intracellular bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of CQDs' distribution in cells by Raman mapping technique
Controllable PL of walnut shell-based CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National key research and development project
National Science and Engineering Research Council of Canada

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by grants from National key research and development project (2016YFB0303201), the Research and Innovation Project of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (No. 14zz069) & Donghua University Graduates' Innovation Funding Projects (No. EG2015006). M.X. would like to thank the Discovery Grant of National Science and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) and NSERC Discovery Accelerator Supplement Award.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Element content changes of walnut shells under various temperatures. Inset: the schematic of chemical structure revolution and the photographs of shells at each stage.
Raman spectra of walnut shells during pre-oxidization and carbonization processes. Inset: the corresponding SEM images.

The microstructural characteristics of walnut shell-based CQDs: (a) HRTEM image, (b) SAED pattern, (c) particle size distribution and (d) lattice spacing of walnut shell-based CQDs. (e) Zigzag edge (f) Armchair edge and (g) defect structure in walnut shell-based CQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of walnut shell-based CQDs. Inset: optical images of CQDs aqueous solution under sunlight (left) and UV light (right). (b) PL emission spectra of CQDs under different excitation wavelength. (c) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs. (d) Effect of dilution ratios on PL properties. Inset: different dilution ratios of CQDs' aqueous solution under sunlight light. (e) PL emission spectra of CQDs prepared at 100 and 140°C. (f) Effect of pH value on the PL intensity of CQDs. Inset: (g) photographs of CQDs under sunlight as a function of pH value.

Effects of alcohol solvents on photoluminescent property of walnut shell-based CQDs: (a) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of initial walnut shell-based CQDs aqueous solution, (b) before and after adding alcohol, (c) removing the upper liquid and (d) adding deionized water again and comparison between initial and final status.

Confocal fluorescent images of walnut shell-based CQDs in MC3T3 cells: (a) DAPI in nucleus; (b) CQDs in cytoplasm; (c) Overlays of the previous two. Raman technique detecting CQDs in MC3T3 cells: (d) Live cellular morphology observed under bright field; Raman intensity signal profile: (e) Given Y position, signal profile at different X positions and (f) given X position, signal profile at different Y positions as depicted in (d).
Schematic illustration of the preparation and application of CQDs from walnut shells.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots from carbonized walnut shells: Structural evolution, fluorescence characteristics, and intracellular bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, Shanghai 201620, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry and Medical Genetics, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Manitoba R3T 5V6, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry and Medical Genetics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, Shanghai 201620, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted tremendous attention for their prominent fluorescence, excellent stability, and outstanding biocompatibility. Herein, we report a facile method to prepare CQDs from walnut shells, which mainly consist of natural cellulose. After carbonization of the walnut shells and acid treatments, zigzag and armchair edges of CQDs with an average size of 3.4nm were revealed by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. Consistent with the (100) planes of graphitic carbon, the lattice spacing distance of these CQDs was 0.258nm. The photoluminescence behaviors related to the size/shape and corresponding edge-state. The CQDs exhibited green fluorescence when the excitation wavelength ranging from 360nm to 460nm. Meanwhile, the CQDs showed hydrophilic, pH-sensitive and up-converted photoluminescence properties. We also found pH and solvent had reversible effects on CQDs' photoluminescence. The CQDs were then tested with live-cell fluorescent images for the potential intracellular probes. Raman mapping technique was also applied to detect the CQDs' distribution in vitro and clarify the entering procedure of CQDs into cells besides confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterials with interesting chemical and physical properties, have attracted increasing interests since their first discovery in 2004 [1–3]. Due to their extraordinary properties, such as facile functionalization, tunable photoluminescence (PL), free of blinking, low photo-bleaching and excellent biocompatibility [4,5], CQDs have shown remarkable potential for a wide range of applications including photo-catalysts [6,7], sensor [8–11], photoelectric devices [12], phototherapy [5,13–15] and bio-imaging [16,17]. To date, many methods and routes have been reported to prepare CQDs [18,19]. However, most of them require complex synthetic conditions, time-consuming post-treatments or costly carbon sources, which limit their further applications. By contrast, chemical oxidation is a facile way to obtain photo-luminescent and colorful CQDs [20]. The fabrication of CQDs from renewable bio-precursors is a challenging concept. Recently, several interesting demonstrations were given to prepare CQDs using inexpensive and biocompatible precursors, like garlic [21], papaya [22], silkworm chrysalis [23], lychee seeds [24], limeade [25] and other carbon-based biomass materials [26–29]. Many of these “green” CQDs without any post-treatment exhibited outstanding performances in biosensing, cellular imaging and chemical sensing applications.

Characterization of the structure transmutation of precursor materials had important significances on the study of PL mechanism of CQDs [30–32]. Raman spectroscopy, which is sensitive to sp2 and sp3 geometries of carbon network, is able to demonstrate the formation of complete carbon network effectively [33,34]. ID/IG, the intensity ratio of two major Raman bands, was proposed as the key parameter to evaluate the microstructure heterogeneity [35,36]. However, no report was presented about the detection of CQDs' distribution in vitro using Raman spectroscopy.

Herein, we report a facile method to prepare photoluminescent CQDs through carbonization combined with chemical cutting from walnut shells, and the resultant CQDs can serve as a staining probe for cellular imaging, as illustrated in Scheme. 1 . The microstructure and chemical composition of the CQDs were analyzed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. In addition, the influencing effects of such environmental factors as pH, temperatures and solvents on CQDs' fluorescence were also investigated by using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Moreover, the application of Raman mapping technique to detect CQDs' distribution in vitro for the very first time.

Walnuts were purchased from local supermarket. 3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other chemical reagents (analytical grade) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used as received without further treatments.

The walnut shells was first washed with water several times and cut into small pieces for efficiency. In a typical procedure, the walnut shells (10g) were immersed in 500mL aqueous solution containing 0.8M sodium chloride and 1M phosphoric acid at room temperature for 24h and then dried in oven at 60°C till reaching weight no further change. Afterwards, the treated shells were pre-oxidized from room temperature to 250°C at a rate of 5°C/min in the carbonizing furnace under air. Nitrogen was then infused into the furnace to prevent oxidization reaction, and the pre-oxidized products were further carbonized at 1000°C. Carbonization procedure was kept 25min to ensure sufficient carbonization. Carbonized resultants (0.3g) were dispersed evenly in 40mL nitric acid and sulfuric acid solution with a volume ratio of 1:3, and then the solution was heated in the oil bath for 12h at 100 and 140°C, respectively. The color of solution changed from basic transparent to a light brown and finally dark brown. After cooling to room temperature, the pH value was adjusted to 7 with 1M NaOH solution in water. Then, uncut structures were removed after being filtered by 0.22μm microporous membrane. Finally, non-carbonaceous ions were removed by dialysis (retained molecular weight: 1000Da). The CQDs solution was stored at 5°C until further characterization and use.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded by JSM-5600(LV) electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) using an accelerating voltage of 10kV. The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were measured by JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan) using an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Fluorescence spectroscopy (FP-6600, JASCO, Japan) and ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrometry (UV–Vis, Lambda 35, PerkinElmer, USA) were used to characterize optical properties. Chemical structures were characterized by Fourier transmission infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher, USA). Raman spectra were obtained using a confocal Raman system (Renishaw InVia Reflex) with a He–Ne laser at a power of 10mW (633nm). Zeta potential (ζ) values of the CQDs were determined with a Zatasizer instrument (Nano-ZS90 analyzer, Malvern, UK).

The 5×104 cells per mL MC3T3 cells seeded in 96-well plate (100μL per well) were cultured for 24h in an incubator (37°C, 5% CO2). Then, the CQDs solution with different concentrations of 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 10μg/mL in α-DMEM was added into well. After certain times (24h or 72h), the culture medium was removed and fresh medium (100μL) containing MTT (10μL, 5mg/mL) was added into each well for 4h further incubating. Then, the culture medium was replaced with 200μL DMSO followed with gentle shake. The absorbance (A) was measured at 490nm with a Microplate Reader Model. The cell viability was estimated according to the following equation.
Cell viability
%
=
A sample
−
A blank
A control
−
A blank
× 100 %
where Asample, Ablank and Acontrol were the absorbance of sample, blank and control, respectively.

In a typical experiment, the 5×104 cells per mL MC3T3 cells seeded in a cover-glass–bottom dish in α-DMEM that contained 10% fetal bovine serum were carefully cultured in incubator (37°C, 5% CO2) for 24h. Then, the cells were cultured for another 24h after the culture medium was replaced with 270μL of α-DMEM containing 30μL CQDs (at the concentration of 1mg/mL). To distinguish the imaging effects of CQDs on different parts in vitro, the nuclei of live cells were stained with DAPI (Best Bio Biotech Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). Finally, the cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) for three times to remove the excess CQDs and DAPI. The images were taken immediately by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, LSM-700, Zeiss, Germany) after incubation and rinsing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CQDs' distribution in cells by Raman mapping technique
ce:section.ce:para: After being cultured in medium containing CQDs (1mg·mL−1) in incubator (37°C, 5% CO2) for 24h, the MC3T3 cells were washed with PBS for three times to remove the excess CQDs. Raman spectroscopy (Invia Reflex, Renishaw, UK) with a He-Ne streamline laser source (633nm, ~4mW) was employed immediately to detect CQDs' distribution in vitro. Raman spectra were obtained by a CCD detector with 1200 grooves/mm and a 100μm×100μm area. 2μm step scanning step was applied in the Raman mapping mode.

High-temperature carbonization can transmute microstructures of raw materials into final CQD products [37]. Main changes in content and color of walnut shells in pre-oxidization and carbonization processes were summarized in Fig. 1 . After 1000°C carbonization, carbon content reached 82%, doubling the raw shell, indicating the microstructures with transformation. Non-carbon elements escaped during the processing, resulting in holes and defects formation (Fig. 1). Raman spectroscopy and SEM showed the original aromatic structure of shells collapsed gradually in high-temperature period. The purpose of heat-treatment was also able to remove the heteroatoms by forming carbon hexagon network simultaneously.

Microstructure transition is a very complicated process. We used Raman spectroscopy to characterize the carbon network structure, especially the characteristic CC vibration peaks of D (sp3 hybrid, 1360cm−1) and G (sp2 hybrid, 1575cm−1) [34]. Fig. 2 presented Raman spectra (red lines) of pre-treated walnut shells at various temperatures and their fitting curves according to Gaussian-Lorentz functions (blue and green lines). During 150–250°C pre-oxidation procedure, embryonic forms of D and G peaks appeared, implying that aromatic carbon rings were ready to form, but high ID/IG values (3.11–3.96) revealed the disorder structures during this period, which could be ascribed to the rapid evolution of byproducts including carbon dioxide and water vapor. The inserted SEM images demonstrated the porous and four-carbon-residue structures gradually formed. Further carbonization at 1000°C, the specimen had smaller ID/IG value (0.92), which indicated that the carbon hexagonal basal planes grow with the increase of temperature. Schematic of chemical structure in Figs. 1 and 2 illustrated the structure revolution during carbonization.

Walnut shell-based CQDs were obtained by chemical cutting carbonized walnut shells in mixed acids conditions at 100°C. HRTEM images (Fig. 3a) indicated that the particles exhibited in the form of quasi-zero dimension and high crystalline with a size distribution between 1 and 10nm (3.4nm average diameter). The average lattice spacing distance of walnut shell-based CQDs was 0.258nm, which was consistent with the (100) planes of graphitic carbon (Fig. 3d). The distinct rings in Fig. 3b indicated that the poly-crystalline forms in walnut shell-based CQDs. It suggests that oxidation cutting process broke the microcrystalline of carbonized shells and CQDs were a mixture of mono- and poly-crystals. In addition, the CQDs exhibited surface defects, and zigzag and armchair edges on the carbon network (Fig. 3e–g).

The PL behaviors can be attributed to the feature of graphene quantum dots, that is, the size/shape and corresponding edge-state [38]. The edge state variations of our CQDs are confirmed by HRTEM as well, showing that the periphery of CQDs consists of mixed zigzag and armchair edges (Fig. 3e and f), although no technical tools can quantify the percentage of the individual edge state. In addition, some defects including imperfect hexagon in the carbon network of CQDs can be discovered (Fig. 3g). Both the edge-state and network defects are associated with the chemical cutting and the precursor shell structure. These structural characteristics of shell-based CQDs result in the anomalous PL phenomena, which will be discussed in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controllable PL of walnut shell-based CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4a, the strongest green fluorescence emission band, at ca. 520nm, was observed under 460nm excitation. Under the irradiation of 365nm UV lamp, the aqueous solution exhibited a green color. An absorption band at ca. 230nm observed in UV–vis spectrum attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and the absorption around 280nm corresponded to n-π* transition of the CO band [39]. Fig. 4b shows the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of the walnut shell-based CQDs. The emission peak gradually shifts to longer wavelengths as the intensity correspondingly increased until reaching the maximum at 520nm obtained at λex =460nm and then gradually decreased. This phenomenon might be due to different particle sizes and various emissive sites on CQDs' surfaces.

The PL mechanism of CQDs is still elusive. Fluorescence emissions due to the presence of surface defects in CQDs were widely accepted [40–42]. Considering that radiative combinations of electron-hole pairs were mainly responsible for fluorescent properties, both surface characteristics and edge structure of a single dot were highlighted, as depicted in Fig. 3e–g. The results suggested that surface defects facilitated more effective radiative combinations of surface-confined electron-holes, and functional groups including carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups at carbon network edges could be regarded as another kind of defect “origin” groups (Fig. 4c). As shown in Fig. 4d, these “origin” groups around basal plane edges, which were considered to be active when being excited by hydrogen photons, were indispensable factors for producing emissive sites since no PL was observed when CQDs were not diluted.

Another popular PL mechanism was proposed based on the quantum confinement effect, that was, the PL was due to the electron transferring from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) [43]. As shown in Fig. 4e, oxidation temperature actually changed the electron transition gap between LUMO and HOMO, leading to the blue shift of the maximum emission band. Additionally, the pH behavior of walnut shell-based CQDs was studied. The CQDs showed increased PL intensity when pH values increased from 1.0 to 5.0, with decreased PL intensity observed when pH>5.0 (Fig. 4f). In order to reveal the particles' colloidal stability and their pH-dependent behavior, we characterized the ζ values of the resulting CQDs under different pH (1−12) conditions (Fig. S1). From pH1 to pH5, the ζ values of CQDs rapidly decreased from 31mV to −32mV; however, from pH6 to pH12, the CQDs had a good colloidal stability without obvious changes in ζ values, which may ascribed to the buffer function of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in alkaline solution [44]. From Fig. S2, we can conclude that the ionic strength of the medium had a minor effect on the PL behavior of walnut shell-based CQDs. The PL intensity was only reduced about 10% with the concentration of NaCl increased from 0 to 3M. In the meantime, the obtained CQDs exhibited good photostability under high UV light, no obvious variation in the fluorescence intensity of walnut shell-based CQDs was observed after irradiation for 6h by using a 500W Xenon lamp (Fig. S3). The photobleaching-resistant property of Walnut shell-based CQDs possibly owing to the electrostatic repulsions between the negatively charged CQDs nanoparticles [45]. These results offer the walnut shell-based CQDs a great potential applications under physiological conditions. More interestingly, as depicted in Fig. S4a, the as-prepared CQDs showed relatively stable up-conversion PL properties, which exhibited the emissions peak from 490 to 500nm when excited by long wavelength light changed from 600 to 800nm. In order to further confirm and explain how these phenomena come from the walnut shell-based CQDs, we introduced an electronic transitions processes model (Fig. S4b). The gap between LOMO and HOMO gradually decreases with the increasing of CQDs size [38], as demonstrated in Fig. S4(b), the up-conversion PL properties could be attributed to the electronic anti-Stokes transition from the energy levels of π to σ, which are originated from the carbine ground-state multiplicity [30]. When some low energy photons, such as excitation light with wavelength >600nm, excite the electrons of the π orbitals, the electrons will transfer to a higher energy state (LOMO), and then the up-converted emission generating as the electrons back to the ground-σ state [46]. This model also explained why the up-converted emission exhibited excitation wavelength dependence feature.

The effects of solvent on fluorescent properties of walnut shell-based CQDs were also investigated. The CQDs could be evenly dispersed in water, however, CQDs in water tended to aggregate when added a few drops of absolute ethanol (a corresponding video is provided in the Supporting Information). It was interesting to notice that CQDs in ethanol showed weak PL, while exhibited high PL as in water when added water again (Fig. 5 ). This distinctive property should be mainly attributed to the abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs particles, which could form strong hydrogen bonds with water. On the contrary, the CQDs displayed poor alcohol solubility. Thus, a certain amount of alcohol could promote the CQDs aggregating and self-quenching owing to its weakened polarity and lowered hydroxyl ratio compared with water (Fig. 5b) [47]. When added water again, the CQDs with lower hydrophilicity (corresponding to lower hydroxyl content) would gradually re-dissolve, and a lower degree of hydroxyl content for the CQDs resulted in the blue-shift of their PL emission (Fig. 5d) [48,49]. The reversible effects of pH and alcohol on walnut shell-based CQDs provided new perspectives for their further investigation in cellular probes and disease diagnosing.

The cytotoxicity of walnut shell-based CQDs was evaluated by the MTT assay with MC3T3 cells. Walnut shell-based CQDs did not show toxicity on MC3T3 cells within a concentration of 100μg/mL and 72h exposure time (Fig. S5), indicating that the walnut shell-based CQDs were safe for bioimaging applications. In order to increase the degree of discrimination between cytoplasm and nucleus, yellow parts were used instead of green emissions from walnut shell-based CQDs upon excitation at 460nm (Fig. 6a–c), and the yellow parts mainly occurred in the areas of cytoplasm, while the PL of walnut shell-based CQDs was very weak inside the cell nucleus. These confocal images demonstrated that the CQDs derived from walnut shells showed stable PL, excellent cyto-compatibility and could serve as a potential probe for cellular imaging and biological fields.

Raman mapping technique was firstly employed to detect CQDs' distribution in cells. As shown in Fig. 6d, the brightness of two dimensional Raman mapping image indicated the position and content of walnut shell-based CQDs in vitro. The nearer the nucleus, the lower the brightness was. The phenomenon indicated that most of CQDs dispersed around nucleus, which was consistent with the results observed from CLSM images. Moreover, it was easy to identify that the intensity of Raman signal varied along the fixed X or Y position (Fig. 6e and f), which implied some CQDs got access into cells by endocytosis gradually. However, there were still residues of CQDs adhering to the surfaces of cell membranes or remaining in the culture medium. It was reasonable to conclude that Raman mapping technique could be an effective method to detect the distribution of CQDs in vitro, which lay foundations for their further applications in biological fields.

In summary, water-dispersible CQDs were obtained from renewable bio-precursors walnut shells via chemical cutting method. The walnut shell-based CQDs, with an average diameter of about 3.4nm, exhibited photostability and photo-luminescence properties. Furthermore, pH and solvents had reversible effects on CQDs' PL. The walnut shell-based CQDs could be effectively used as bioimaging reagents for intracellular trafficking, which co-verified along with Raman mapping technique. These CQDs show promising in bioimaging and disease diagnosing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by grants from National key research and development project (2016YFB0303201), the Research and Innovation Project of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (No. 14zz069) & Donghua University Graduates' Innovation Funding Projects (No. EG2015006). M.X. would like to thank the Discovery Grant of National Science and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) and NSERC Discovery Accelerator Supplement Award.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.05.094.
